text.title: direnv + mise: Concepts
text.summary: direnv watches directory changes and loads/unloads .envrc. mise manages runtime versions (Python, Node, etc.) per directory via .tool-versions or mise.toml. Together: cd into a project → correct Python version activates + venv loads + .env variables set automatically.

rule.statement: Run `direnv allow` once in every directory with a new or modified .envrc before it executes.
rule.rationale: direnv runs arbitrary shell code from .envrc. Without explicit trust, cloning a repo with a malicious .envrc would execute code on cd. This is the key security mechanism.

example.code: cd ~/workspace/repos/my-project direnv allow # first time, or after any .envrc change # direnv: loading .envrc # direnv: export +VIRTUAL_ENV ~PATH

section.title: mise as asdf Replacement
section.p: mise replaces pyenv, nvm, and asdf with a single tool. Reads .tool-versions (asdf-compatible) or mise.toml. Integrates with direnv via `use mise` directive in .envrc.

list.item: `mise install python@3.12.8` — download and compile Python 3.12.8
`mise use python@3.12.8` — set version for current directory (writes .tool-versions)
`mise current` — show active versions in current directory

rule.statement: mise hook must appear BEFORE direnv hook in .bashrc. direnv must be the LAST hook in .bashrc.
rule.rationale: direnv re-evaluates the shell environment on every directory change. If mise activates after direnv, the runtime versions set by mise won't be visible to direnv's `use mise` directive.

example.code: # ~/.bashrc — correct order eval "$(mise activate bash)" # 1. mise first eval "$(direnv hook bash)" # 2. direnv LAST
# Wrong order — `use mise` in .envrc won't find mise-managed runtimes eval "$(direnv hook bash)" eval "$(mise activate bash)"
